content.body: Heat energy transfers from regions of higher temperature to lower temperature by three methods: CONDUCTION, CONVECTION, and RADIATION.
CONDUCTION: Transfer through direct contact. Particles vibrate and pass energy to neighbours. Best in solids (especially metals). Mechanism: free electrons in metals carry energy quickly.
CONVECTION: Transfer by movement of a fluid (liquid or gas). Heated fluid expands, becomes less dense, rises. Cooler, denser fluid sinks to replace it — forming convection currents.

RADIATION: Transfer as infrared electromagnetic waves. Does NOT need a medium — works in a vacuum (how we receive heat from the Sun). Dark/matt surfaces are best absorbers and emitters. Light/shiny surfaces are poor absorbers (good reflectors).

content.title: Real-life Examples
content.body:
Conduction: Metal spoon heats up in hot soup
Convection: Sea breeze (land heats faster than sea during day)
Radiation: Sun's heat reaching Earth through space

questions.question: Which method of heat transfer does NOT need a medium (can occur in a vacuum)?
questions.explanation: Radiation travels as electromagnetic waves — it needs no particles, so it crosses a vacuum (like space).

questions.question: Metals are good conductors of heat because:
options: Free electrons carry thermal energy quickly
questions.answer: Free electrons carry thermal energy quickly
questions.explanation: Metals have delocalised (free) electrons that rapidly transfer kinetic energy through the material.

questions.question: Convection currents occur because hot fluids:
questions.explanation: Hot fluid expands → lower density → rises. Cool fluid is denser → sinks. This creates circulation.

questions.question: A black, matt surface compared to a white, shiny surface will:
questions.explanation: Dark matt surfaces are best absorbers AND best emitters of infrared radiation.

questions.question: Heat transfer in liquids and gases is mainly by:
questions.explanation: Liquids and gases are poor conductors — heat mainly transfers by bulk movement (convection currents).

questions.question: Why does a metal feel colder than wood at the same room temperature?
options: Metal conducts heat away from skin faster
questions.answer: Metal conducts heat away from skin faster
questions.explanation: Both are at the same temperature, but metal is a better conductor — it draws heat from your skin faster, feeling colder.

questions.question: A vacuum flask reduces heat transfer by:
questions.explanation: Vacuum flask: silvered walls (reduce radiation), vacuum between walls (no conduction/convection), stopper (reduces conduction/convection at top).

questions.question: Sea breeze during the day blows from:
questions.explanation: Land heats faster → hot air rises over land → cool sea air moves in to replace it = sea breeze blows onshore.

questions.question: The Sun heats Earth by:
questions.explanation: There is a vacuum (space) between Sun and Earth — only radiation can cross it.

questions.question: Wool clothing keeps you warm because:
options: Wool traps air — poor conductor — reduces heat loss
questions.answer: Wool traps air — poor conductor — reduces heat loss
questions.explanation: Air is an excellent insulator. Trapped air in wool fibres prevents convection and conduction heat loss.

questions.question: Hot water pipes have fins to:
options: Increase surface area for better convection cooling
questions.answer: Increase surface area for better convection cooling
questions.explanation: More surface area → more contact with air → more heat lost by convection and radiation.

questions.question: The rate of conduction depends on:
options: Temperature difference, cross-section area, material type, thickness
questions.answer: Temperature difference, cross-section area, material type, thickness
questions.explanation: Rate of conduction ∝ ΔT × A ÷ thickness. Material type (conductivity) also matters.

questions.question: Which is the best emitter of infrared radiation?
questions.explanation: Matt black surfaces are the best emitters AND absorbers of radiation. Shiny surfaces are poor emitters.

questions.question: In central heating, water rises up pipes and hot air rises in rooms. This is:
questions.explanation: Hot water/air naturally rises (less dense), cool sinks — natural convection currents distribute heat.

questions.question: Why are cavity walls in houses filled with foam or mineral wool?
options: To trap air and reduce convection currents
questions.answer: To trap air and reduce convection currents
questions.explanation: Foam/wool prevents air movement in cavity — stops convection. Still air is a good insulator.

questions.question: Radiation travels at:
questions.explanation: Infrared radiation is electromagnetic — it travels at the speed of light in vacuum.

questions.question: Convection cannot occur in:
questions.explanation: Convection requires fluid (liquid or gas) that can flow. Solid particles are fixed — they cannot move to create currents.

questions.question: A solar water heater uses dark pipes because dark surfaces:
questions.explanation: Dark surfaces absorb more incoming infrared radiation — more efficient at collecting solar energy.

questions.question: Thermal conductivity of air compared to glass is:
questions.explanation: Air is a poor conductor (good insulator). This is why double glazing (air gap) reduces heat loss.

questions.question: Which method of heat transfer involves actual movement of matter?
questions.explanation: Convection involves bulk movement of fluid particles carrying heat energy. Conduction/radiation do not move matter.
